← all tutorials

Sequential Consistency — interactive SEQUENTIAL_CONSISTENCY.md sequential_consistency.py

All operations appear to execute in some sequential order that preserves each process's program order — but with NO real-time guarantee. Drag the chips to build your own total order, then flip the model: sequential (program order only) vs linearizable (add real time). Watch the verdict flip on the stale read (HIST_A). The four histories are recomputed live from the identical data as sequential_consistency.py.

① Two-process timeline — invocation/response intervals & program order

What to do: pick a history. Each bar spans an operation's [inv, resp]. Green arrows are PROGRAM ORDER (same process, always locked). Tick real-time arrows to see the ordering that linearizability ADDS on top — those are exactly what sequential consistency ignores.
write read program order (locked) real-time (ignored by seq) initial X = 0

② Build the script — drag the chips; does it preserve program order & read back legally?

What to do: drag the chips left/right — their left-to-right order is your candidate total order (script). The replay trace recomputes instantly. A script is valid for the active model when it (a) preserves program order, (b) is a legal register trace, and — if linearizable — also (c) respects real time. For HIST_A, drag R3 in front of W1 to make the stale read legal under sequential; switch to linearizable and it breaks.
drag a chip…

③ Model toggle & checker — enumerate orderings, test each

What this shows: the checker enumerates every total order consistent with the active model's partial order (topological sorts) and replays each as a register. If any is legal ⟹ the history satisfies the model. Switch the model: sequential consistency uses program order (fewer edges, more freedom); linearizability adds real time (strictly stronger). HIST_A flips from YES to NO.

④ The consistency hierarchy — strict > linearizable > sequential > causal > eventual

Where it sits: each rung DROPS a constraint going down. Sequential consistency keeps a single global order (stronger than causal/eventual) but drops real time (weaker than linearizable/strict). That is why it's cheap (snooping cache) while linearizability needs consensus.
check: … JS checker matches sequential_consistency.py: A=YES, B=YES, C=YES, D=NO (seq); A is NOT linearizable.
